expanded_description: Understanding reflections as rigid transformations that flip figures over a line of reflection, preserving distance and angle measure while reversing orientation, and exploring line symmetry in geometric and real-world contexts

core_concept.principle: A reflection maps every point of a figure to an image point such that the line of reflection is the perpendicular bisector of the segment connecting each preimage point to its image point
core_concept.explanation: Reflections create mirror images across a fixed line. The distance from any point to the line of reflection equals the distance from its image to that line. While reflections preserve size, shape, and angle measures, they reverse the orientation (clockwise becomes counterclockwise), distinguishing them from translations and rotations.

image.alt: Triangle reflected across a vertical line showing perpendicular bisectors and equal distances
image.caption: Figure 1: The line of reflection is the perpendicular bisector of every segment connecting a point to its image

sections.introduction: The x-axis and y-axis serve as the most common lines of reflection in coordinate geometry. Each axis has a simple, consistent rule for transforming coordinates.

key_concept.definition: Reflecting across the x-axis changes the sign of the y-coordinate: $(x,y) \to (x,-y)$. Reflecting across the y-axis changes the sign of the x-coordinate: $(x,y) \to (-x,y)$
key_concept.context: These rules work because the axis of reflection acts as a mirror, keeping the coordinate parallel to the axis unchanged while negating the perpendicular coordinate

image.alt: Point reflected across x-axis and y-axis with coordinate rules labeled
image.caption: Figure 2: Axis reflection rules preserve one coordinate and negate the other
Figure 3: Vertical flip changes only the y-value
Figure 4: Horizontal flip changes only the x-value

principles.statement: When reflecting over an axis, the coordinate perpendicular to that axis changes sign while the parallel coordinate remains identical
principles.application: Quickly find image coordinates without plotting by applying the sign-change rule
principles.example: Reflect $A(-4,-3)$ over x-axis: x stays -4, y becomes 3 $\to A'(-4,3)$
principles.analogy: Like looking in a mirror placed along the axis—your distance to the mirror stays the same, but you're now on the opposite side

worked_examples.title: Reflecting a Triangle Over the x-axis
worked_examples.problem: Triangle $JKL$ has vertices $J(2,4)$, $K(-1,5)$, and $L(3,1)$. Reflect $\triangle JKL$ across the x-axis. List the coordinates of $\triangle J'K'L'$.

sections.introduction: Figures can be reflected across any line, not just the coordinate axes. Common special lines include $y=x$, $y=-x$, and vertical/horizontal lines like $x=a$ or $y=b$.

key_concept.definition: For any line of reflection, each point and its image are equidistant from the line, and the segment connecting them is perpendicular to the line
key_concept.context: Special diagonal lines have coordinate-swapping rules: $y=x$ swaps coordinates, $y=-x$ swaps and negates both

image.alt: Point reflected across line y=x showing coordinate swap
image.caption: Figure 5: Reflection over $y=x$ exchanges x and y coordinates

principles.application: Use these shortcuts for quick coordinate transformations without geometric construction
principles.analogy: Like folding paper along a diagonal crease—the coordinates trade places or flip signs depending on the fold angle

principles.statement: The line of reflection is the locus of points equidistant from every preimage-image pair
principles.application: Construct reflections by dropping perpendiculars to the line and marking equal distances on the opposite side
principles.example: To reflect over $x=3$, find horizontal distance $|x-3|$, then place image at $3 \pm |x-3|$ on the opposite side
principles.analogy: Like a tightrope walker stepping the same distance to either side of the center rope

worked_examples.title: Reflection Over Vertical Line x = a
worked_examples.problem: Reflect quadrilateral $ABCD$ with vertices $A(-4,2)$, $B(-2,5)$, $C(1,3)$, $D(-1,-1)$ across the line $x=1$. Find the image coordinates.

step_by_step.step: Establish reflection formula for vertical line
step_by_step.explanation: For line $x=a$, image x-coordinate is $2a - x_{original}$

image.alt: Quadrilateral reflected across vertical line x=1 with distances marked
image.caption: Figure 6: Points on the line of reflection remain fixed

worked_examples.title: Reflection Over y = x
worked_examples.problem: Reflect triangle $PQR$ with $P(0,3)$, $Q(2,1)$, $R(-1,-2)$ over the line $y=x$. Verify orientation reversal.

step_by_step.explanation: Trace $P \to Q \to R$: moves down-right, then down-left (counterclockwise)
Trace $P' \to Q' \to R'$: moves left-up, then left-down (clockwise)

worked_examples.final_answer: $P'(3,0), Q'(1,2), R'(-2,-1)$. Orientation reversed from counterclockwise to clockwise.
worked_examples.concept_applied: y=x reflection swaps coordinates and reverses orientation

image.alt: Triangle reflected over y=x showing coordinate swap and orientation flip
image.caption: Figure 7: Reflection always reverses vertex order direction

process_flow.description: Determine if it's an axis, diagonal, or arbitrary line $x=a$ or $y=b$
process_flow.mechanism: Write line in standard or slope-intercept form

process_flow.description: For each vertex, find the shortest distance to the line
applications: Diagonal lines: use perpendicular slope or formula

process_flow.description: Extend the perpendicular the same distance $d$ to the opposite side of the line
Draw image polygon, confirm distances to line are equal, check orientation reversal
process_flow.organization: Verify corresponding segments are perpendicular to line of reflection

sections.introduction: A figure has line symmetry if it can be folded along a line so that the two halves match exactly. This property appears extensively in nature, art, architecture, and mathematics.

key_concept.definition: A line of symmetry is a line that divides a figure into two congruent halves that are mirror images of each other
key_concept.context: The number of lines of symmetry varies by shape; regular polygons have symmetry lines equal to their number of sides

image.alt: Equilateral triangle, square, and circle showing multiple lines of symmetry
image.caption: Figure 8: Regular polygons have symmetry lines equal to their side count

categories.description: Symmetry lines parallel to the coordinate axes, common in rectangles, isosceles triangles, and letters like A, B, H
categories.detailed_mechanism: Folding along the axis creates perfect overlap of left/right or top/bottom halves

examples.explanation: Two lines: vertical through center, horizontal through center

image.alt: Rectangle with two perpendicular symmetry lines
image.caption: Figure 9: Rectangles have exactly 2 lines of symmetry

examples.explanation: One vertical line through the midpoints of parallel bases

image.alt: Trapezoid with single vertical line of symmetry
image.caption: Figure 10: Only one line divides it into matching halves

categories.description: Symmetry lines that run corner-to-corner, found in squares, rhombuses, and kites
categories.detailed_mechanism: Reflection across the diagonal maps vertices and sides onto corresponding positions

examples.explanation: Four lines: vertical, horizontal, and two diagonals

image.alt: Square with four lines of symmetry marked
image.caption: Figure 11: Squares have maximum symmetry among quadrilaterals

categories.description: Figures with multiple lines of symmetry often exhibit rotational symmetry; each symmetry line passes through the center of rotation
categories.detailed_mechanism: For regular n-gons, lines of symmetry intersect at the centroid, creating equal angular spacing of $180°/n$

examples.explanation: Six lines: three through opposite vertices, three through midpoints of opposite sides

image.alt: Regular hexagon with six symmetry lines
image.caption: Figure 12: Regular n-gons have exactly n lines of symmetry

principles.statement: A regular polygon with n sides has exactly n lines of symmetry
principles.application: Predict symmetry lines without drawing by knowing the number of sides
principles.example: Regular pentagon: 5 lines; Regular octagon: 8 lines; Circle: infinite lines
principles.analogy: Like spokes on a wheel—each symmetry line radiates from the center to a vertex or midpoint

worked_examples.title: Identifying Lines of Symmetry in Polygons
worked_examples.problem: Determine the number of lines of symmetry for each figure: (a) Equilateral triangle, (b) Isosceles triangle, (c) Scalene triangle, (d) Regular decagon.

step_by_step.explanation: Regular 3-gon; each altitude is a symmetry line
Only the altitude from vertex angle to base creates mirror halves
All sides/angles different; no fold creates matching halves

worked_examples.concept_applied: Symmetry count depends on regularity and side/angle equality

worked_examples.title: Finding Missing Coordinates Using Symmetry
worked_examples.problem: Rectangle $MNOP$ has vertices $M(-3,4)$, $N(5,4)$, and $P(-3,-2)$. The rectangle is symmetric about the lines $x=1$ and $y=1$. Find vertex $O$ using symmetry.

step_by_step.explanation: Center of rectangle is at intersection of symmetry lines: $(1,1)$

step_by_step.step: Use diagonal symmetry or coordinate pairing
step_by_step.explanation: Opposite vertices are symmetric about center: midpoint of $M$ and $O$ is $(1,1)$
Reflect $M(-3,4)$ over $x=1 \to (5,4) = N$. Reflect $N(5,4)$ over $y=1 \to (5,-2) = O$. Correct.
step_by_step.calculation: Consistent with both lines of symmetry

worked_examples.concept_applied: Symmetry lines pass through center; opposite points reflect across both axes

summary: A reflection is a rigid transformation that flips a figure over a line of reflection, creating a mirror image. The line of reflection serves as the perpendicular bisector of every segment connecting a preimage point to its image point, preserving distances and angle measures while reversing orientation. Key coordinate rules include: x-axis reflection $(x,y) \to (x,-y)$, y-axis reflection $(x,y) \to (-x,y)$, $y=x$ reflection $(x,y) \to (y,x)$, and $y=-x$ reflection $(x,y) \to (-y,-x)$. For arbitrary lines like $x=a$ or $y=b$, the formula $x' = 2a - x$ or $y' = 2b - y$ applies. Line symmetry (reflectional symmetry) occurs when a figure can be folded along a line so both halves match exactly. Regular polygons have exactly $n$ lines of symmetry, where $n$ is the number of sides. Rectangles have 2, rhombuses have 2, squares have 4, and circles have infinitely many. Understanding reflections builds foundational skills for symmetry analysis, coordinate geometry proofs, optical design, and pattern recognition in mathematics and nature.
